name: Mongolia
tagline: From steppe horse-archers to the largest land empire in history, and the long road back to an independent democracy.
summary: Mongolia is the heartland of the great nomadic confederations of the Eurasian steppe, from the Xiongnu of the third century BCE to the empire forged by Temüjin, who in 1206 was proclaimed Chinggis (Genghis) Khan, supreme ruler of a united Mongol nation. His successors built the largest contiguous land empire ever known and, under his grandson Kublai Khan, ruled China as the Yuan dynasty. After the empire fragmented, Mongolia fell under the overlordship of the Manchu Qing for two centuries and embraced Tibetan Buddhism. Independence declared in 1911 and secured by revolution in 1921 produced the Soviet-aligned Mongolian People's Republic in 1924; a peaceful revolution in 1990 ended one-party rule, and Mongolia today is a multiparty democracy and market economy wedged between Russia and China.

founding.label: Chinggis (Genghis) Khan unites the Mongol nation
founding.detail: Mongolia's founding moment is dated to 1206, when an assembly (khuriltai) of steppe leaders gathered by the River Onon proclaimed Temüjin as Chinggis Khan, 'Universal Ruler,' uniting the warring tribes of the Mongolian plateau into a single nation under one law, the Yassa. The modern state traces its sovereignty to the declaration of independence from China in 1911 and the proclamation of the Mongolian People's Republic in November 1924, with the present democratic constitution adopted in 1992.

eras.title: Lords of the Steppe: The Xiongnu and Early Nomads
eras.standfirst: Long before the Mongols, mounted confederations rose and fell on the grasslands, rivalling the empires of China.
body: The grasslands of present-day Mongolia were the cradle of a succession of mobile, horse-borne societies whose wealth lay in herds rather than fields. The first to leave a clear historical record were the Xiongnu, a tribal confederation that, at the very end of the third century BCE, attained an unmatched formidability under a supreme ruler titled the chanyu, a steppe counterpart to the Chinese 'son of heaven.' For centuries the Xiongnu pressed against the newly unified Qin and Han empires to the south, a rivalry that helped drive the building of China's great frontier walls. The Mongols would later regard the Xiongnu as their remote ancestors.

After several centuries of warfare with China, complicated by their own civil wars, the Xiongnu confederation broke apart, with some southern tribes settling within China and others dispersing across Inner Asia by the first century CE. The political vacuum on the steppe was filled by a sequence of further confederations and empires, including the Xianbei, the Rouran, and the Turkic khaganates, each rising to dominate the grasslands and then giving way to a rival. Power on the steppe was unstable by nature: it depended on a charismatic ruler's ability to bind clans together, and it tended to dissolve when that personal authority lapsed.

Among these successors the Uyghurs and other Turkic peoples left a lasting imprint on the Orkhon River valley, the symbolic political heartland of the eastern steppe, where they built the city of Khar Balgas. The valley's pastures, watered by the Orkhon, sustained nomadic society for two millennia and would later host the imperial capital of the Mongols themselves. By the early second millennium CE the region was a patchwork of Mongol- and Turkic-speaking tribes, including the Tatars, Kereit, Naiman, and Merkit, feuding for grazing land, livestock, and prestige, a fractured world awaiting a unifier.

events.title: The Xiongnu confederation reaches its height
events.summary: At the close of the third century BCE the Xiongnu unite the eastern steppe under a supreme chanyu, forming a nomadic empire that rivals the Qin and Han dynasties of China for centuries.

events.title: Break-up of the Xiongnu empire
events.summary: After prolonged war with China and internal division, the Xiongnu confederation fractures; some tribes are absorbed into China while others scatter, ending the first great steppe empire centred on Mongolia.

events.title: Uyghur khaganate in the Orkhon valley
events.summary: Turkic Uyghurs establish a khaganate centred on the Orkhon River, building the city of Khar Balgas; the valley remains the political and ceremonial heart of the eastern steppe.

pullquote.text: The Xiongnu are thought by the Mongols to be their remote ancestors.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the early peoples of the steppe

eras.title: Chinggis Khan and the Birth of the Mongol Nation
eras.standfirst: An orphaned tribal outcast bound the feuding clans into one people and launched conquests that reshaped Eurasia.
body: Temüjin was born around 1162 into a clan of the Mongol tribe near Lake Baikal. After his father Yesügei was poisoned, the boy and his family were abandoned by their followers and reduced to destitution; he survived captivity and hardship to rebuild a base of loyal companions. Through a long series of alliances, betrayals, and wars, he subdued the rival Tatars, Kereit, Naiman, and Merkit, breaking the old clan nobility and reorganizing his followers into a disciplined military society loyal to him personally rather than to their tribes. By the opening years of the thirteenth century he had no remaining rival on the Mongolian plateau.

In 1206 a great assembly of leaders convened by the River Onon proclaimed Temüjin as Chinggis Khan, a title generally understood to mean 'Universal Ruler.' This was the founding act of the Mongol nation: for the first time the scattered tribes of the steppe were welded into a single people under one sovereign and one body of law, the Yassa, which demanded absolute obedience, the unity of the tribes, and merciless punishment of wrongdoers. Chinggis organized his subjects into a decimal military system and a corps of personal guards, creating an instrument of conquest of unprecedented discipline and reach.

From this base Chinggis turned outward. His armies struck the Tangut kingdom of Xixia and the Jin dynasty of northern China, then swept west to shatter the wealthy empire of Khwarazm in Central Asia, while his generals raided into Persia and the Russian lands. The campaigns were devastatingly destructive, with later estimates attributing tens of millions of deaths to the Mongol wars. Chinggis Khan died in August 1227 during a final campaign against Xixia, leaving an empire that already stretched from the Pacific approaches to the Caspian Sea and a succession arrangement dividing the realm among his four sons.

events.title: Temüjin proclaimed Chinggis Khan
events.summary: An assembly of steppe leaders by the River Onon proclaims Temüjin as Chinggis Khan, 'Universal Ruler,' uniting the Mongol tribes into one nation governed by the Yassa law code, the founding act of the Mongol state.

events.title: Conquest of the Khwarazmian empire
events.summary: Mongol armies invade and destroy the powerful Khwarazmian empire of Central Asia, opening the way to Persia and the Russian steppe and demonstrating the reach of the new Mongol military.

events.title: Death of Chinggis Khan
events.summary: Chinggis Khan dies in August 1227 during a campaign against the Tangut Xixia kingdom, leaving an empire reaching from northern China to the Caspian Sea and a realm divided among his four sons.

figures.name: Chinggis (Genghis) Khan
figures.role: Founder and first Great Khan of the Mongol Empire
figures.blurb: Born Temüjin, he united the feuding tribes of the steppe and was proclaimed Chinggis Khan in 1206, founding the Mongol nation under the Yassa law. His armies conquered the Jin, Tangut, and Khwarazmian states, beginning the largest contiguous land empire in history.

pullquote.text: An assembly of leaders declared him universal emperor (chinggis khān) of the Mongolian steppe.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the events of 1206

eras.title: The Great Empire: Karakorum, Kublai, and the Yuan
eras.standfirst: Chinggis Khan's heirs raised a steppe capital, conquered China, and ruled an empire spanning a continent.
body: Under Chinggis Khan's son and successor Ögödei, elected great khan in 1229, the empire continued to expand and acquired a fixed seat of power. From 1235 Ögödei walled the city of Karakorum in the Orkhon valley and raised a great columned palace there, making it the administrative heart of an empire that stretched across Asia. Mongol armies under Ögödei and the later khans overran the remaining Jin territory, swept through the Russian principalities, and pushed into Central Europe and the Middle East, while a system of relay stations and protected trade routes knit the vast realm together and revived long-distance commerce across Eurasia.

The empire's very size strained its unity. After the reign of Möngke, the last great khan to rule from Karakorum, the realm effectively fragmented into competing khanates: the Golden Horde in the Russian steppe, the Ilkhanate in Persia, the Chagatai khanate in Central Asia, and the domain of the great khan in the east. In this division, Chinggis Khan's grandson Kublai Khan emerged dominant in the east. Kublai completed the Mongol conquest of China, overcoming the Southern Song by 1279, and in 1271 proclaimed the Yuan dynasty, becoming the first foreign ruler of all China.

Kublai moved the centre of his power from Karakorum to Khanbaliq, modern Beijing, around 1267, shifting the empire's gravity from the steppe into China. The Yuan governed a vast, multiethnic state and reopened China to overland exchange, the era in which the Venetian Marco Polo claimed to have travelled. Mongol rule rested on a small foreign elite over a far larger Chinese population, however, and was undermined by factional strife, fiscal crisis, and natural disaster. In 1368 the Chinese rebel Zhu Yuanzhang expelled the Mongols and founded the Ming dynasty; the last Yuan emperor fled north into the steppes, ending Mongol rule over China after less than a century.

events.title: Karakorum becomes the imperial capital
events.summary: Great Khan Ögödei walls the city of Karakorum in the Orkhon valley and builds a palace there, establishing a fixed capital for the Mongol Empire on the Mongolian steppe.

events.title: Kublai Khan founds the Yuan dynasty
events.summary: Chinggis Khan's grandson Kublai proclaims the Yuan dynasty in 1271 and completes the conquest of China by 1279, ruling all of China from Khanbaliq (Beijing) as its first non-Chinese sovereign.

events.title: Fall of the Yuan and retreat to the steppe
events.summary: The Chinese rebel Zhu Yuanzhang overthrows the Yuan and founds the Ming dynasty; the last Mongol emperor flees north into the steppes, ending Mongol rule over China.

figures.name: Kublai Khan
figures.role: Great Khan and founder of the Yuan dynasty
figures.blurb: Grandson of Chinggis Khan who completed the Mongol conquest of China and proclaimed the Yuan dynasty in 1271, ruling a Eurasian empire from Khanbaliq (Beijing). He was the first non-Chinese ruler to govern all of China.

figures.name: Ögödei Khan
figures.role: Second Great Khan of the Mongol Empire
figures.blurb: Son and successor of Chinggis Khan, elected great khan in 1229, who built the capital at Karakorum from 1235 and directed the empire's expansion deep into China, the Middle East, and Europe.

pullquote.text: Karakorum was the capital of the Mongol empire, in the valley of the Orhon (Orkhon) River.

eras.title: Buddhism and the Manchu Overlordship
eras.standfirst: After empire came fragmentation, the conversion to Tibetan Buddhism, and two centuries of Qing rule.
body: The collapse of the Yuan threw the Mongols back upon their steppe homeland and into renewed division among rival khans and confederations of the eastern and western Mongols. Repeated attempts to restore a single Mongol authority largely failed, and political fragmentation became the defining condition of the post-imperial centuries. Amid this disunity a profound cultural transformation took hold: from the later sixteenth century the Mongols increasingly embraced Tibetan Buddhism of the Gelug ('Yellow Hat') school, which would reshape their society, art, and institutions for the next four hundred years.

The new faith acquired a Mongolian focus in the person of Zanabazar. In 1639 a son of the Tüshetü Khan of the Khalkha was recognized as a reincarnation and enthroned in 1640 with the title Javzandamba khutagt, becoming the first Bogd Gegeen, the supreme Buddhist authority in Mongolia. A celebrated sculptor and scholar as well as a religious leader, Zanabazar gave the Mongol church its own incarnate head and helped bind Buddhist devotion to Mongol identity. The line of the Javzandamba khutagts he founded would endure for centuries and ultimately furnish Mongolia's head of state.

Political independence, however, was lost. The Manchus, who founded the Qing dynasty over China, incorporated the Mongols into their empire during the seventeenth century, the Khalkha of the north submitting in 1691. To secure their hold the Qing applied careful religious controls, ruling that no descendant of Chinggis Khan could be recognized as a living Buddha and that the reincarnations of the Javzandamba must be sought in Tibet rather than among the Mongol nobility. For more than two centuries Mongolia remained under Manchu overlordship, divided administratively and governed through its native nobility and its monasteries, until the Qing itself entered terminal crisis in the early twentieth century.

events.title: Zanabazar enthroned as first Javzandamba
events.summary: Zanabazar, recognized as a reincarnation in 1639, is enthroned with the title Javzandamba khutagt, becoming the first Bogd Gegeen and supreme head of Tibetan Buddhism in Mongolia.

events.title: Khalkha Mongols submit to the Qing
events.summary: The Khalkha Mongols of the north accept Manchu overlordship, bringing Mongolia under the Qing dynasty; the Manchus govern through the Mongol nobility and impose religious controls to secure their rule.

figures.name: Zanabazar (Öndör Gegeen)
figures.blurb: Son of a Khalkha Tüshetü Khan recognized as a Buddhist reincarnation and enthroned in 1640 as the first Bogd Gegeen of Mongolia. A master sculptor whose images of the goddess Tara survive in Ulaanbaatar, he gave the Mongol church its own incarnate head.

pullquote.text: Zanabazar was enthroned in 1640 with the title Javzandamba khutagt and proclaimed Öndör Geegen.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the first Bogd Gegeen

eras.title: Independence, Revolution, and the People's Republic
eras.standfirst: A theocratic monarchy, a revolution backed by Soviet bayonets, and seven decades as a satellite of Moscow.
body: When the Chinese revolution toppled the Qing in 1911, unrest spread through Mongolia. In December the Manchu official was expelled, and the Javzandamba was proclaimed the Bogd Khan, 'Holy King,' as head of an independent theocratic monarchy. Full sovereignty proved elusive: only autonomy under Chinese suzerainty was internationally recognized, and in the chaos of the following decade Chinese forces reoccupied the capital and were in turn driven out by the White Russian adventurer Baron Ungern-Sternberg, who seized Niislel Khüree (now Ulaanbaatar) in 1921.

Into this turmoil stepped a small revolutionary movement. Damdin Sükhbaatar, a former soldier, helped found the Mongolian People's Party, sought aid in Soviet Russia, and in 1921 led a People's Army that, alongside the Soviet Red Army, expelled Ungern-Sternberg's forces and captured the capital in July. A people's government was installed under the nominal authority of the Bogd Khan. When the Bogd Khan died in May 1924, the monarchy lapsed, and in November 1924 the Mongolian People's Republic was proclaimed, the world's second socialist state after the Soviet Union.

For the next sixty-five years Mongolia was a closely aligned satellite of Moscow. In the late 1930s the regime under Khorloogiin Choibalsan, prompted by Soviet advisers, carried out the 'Great Repression,' in which tens of thousands of people were killed, including many thousands of Buddhist lamas, and the monasteries that had dominated Mongolian life were almost entirely destroyed. Mongolia fought alongside Soviet forces against Japan, and after 1945 its independence won wider recognition; it joined the United Nations in 1961. A one-party state on the Soviet model, it remained economically and politically bound to Moscow until the late 1980s.

events.title: Independence declared under the Bogd Khan
events.summary: As the Qing collapses, Mongolia expels the Manchu official and proclaims the Javzandamba as Bogd Khan, head of an independent theocratic monarchy, though only autonomy under Chinese suzerainty is recognized abroad.

events.title: People's Revolution captures the capital
events.summary: Damdin Sükhbaatar's People's Army, with the Soviet Red Army, drives out the White Russian forces of Baron Ungern-Sternberg and takes Niislel Khüree in July 1921, installing a people's government.

events.title: Mongolian People's Republic proclaimed
events.summary: After the death of the Bogd Khan in May 1924, the monarchy lapses and the Mongolian People's Republic is proclaimed in November, the world's second socialist state and a satellite of the Soviet Union.

events.title: The Great Repression
events.summary: Under Choibalsan and Soviet NKVD advisers, the state carries out mass purges, killing tens of thousands of people, executing thousands of lamas, and destroying hundreds of Buddhist monasteries.

events.title: Mongolia admitted to the United Nations
events.summary: Mongolia is admitted to the United Nations in 1961, broadening international recognition of its independence after decades in which its sovereignty had been disputed between its powerful neighbours.

figures.name: Damdin Sükhbaatar
figures.role: Revolutionary leader and founder of the People's Republic
figures.blurb: A former soldier and co-founder of the Mongolian People's Party who organized the People's Army that, with Soviet help, captured the capital in 1921 and laid the foundation for the Mongolian People's Republic. The capital's central square is named for him.

figures.name: Bogd Khan (the eighth Javzandamba)
figures.blurb: The eighth Javzandamba khutagt, proclaimed Bogd Khan when Mongolia declared independence in 1911. He reigned as the spiritual and nominal political head of state until his death in May 1924, after which the monarchy was abolished and the People's Republic proclaimed.

pullquote.text: In 1921 the city was occupied by troops of Mongolia's revolutionary leader, Damdiny Sühbaatar, and the Soviet Red Army.

eras.title: Democratic Mongolia
eras.standfirst: A peaceful revolution ended one-party rule; a young democracy now navigates a mining boom between two giant neighbours.
body: As Soviet reform and the collapse of communism rippled across the bloc, Mongolians took to the streets. In the winter of 1989–90 demonstrations and hunger strikes in the capital pressed the ruling Mongolian People's Revolutionary Party for change, and in March 1990 the party's leadership resigned, opening the way to peaceful democratic transition. Constitutional amendments legalized opposition parties and provided for multiparty elections; new parties, including the Mongolian Democratic Party and Mongolian Social Democratic Party, registered to contest the first free elections held that year.

A new democratic constitution, adopted in 1992, transformed the country from a 'people's republic' into a multiparty parliamentary state with an elected president, a unicameral State Great Khural, and guarantees of civil liberties, and committed Mongolia to building a market economy. The transition from central planning brought a sharp recession in the early 1990s before growth resumed. Power has since alternated peacefully between the renamed former ruling party and democratic opposition coalitions, making Mongolia one of the few enduring democracies in its region.

From the early 2000s the discovery and development of vast mineral deposits, above all the Oyu Tolgoi copper and gold mine, drove a mining boom that multiplied Mongolia's income, reshaped its economy away from herding, and drew rural migrants to a fast-growing Ulaanbaatar. The wealth has been contested, prompting disputes over foreign investment terms, environmental damage, and corruption, and recent years have seen episodic protests and concerns about the resilience of democratic freedoms. Landlocked between Russia and China, Mongolia in 2026 pursues a 'third neighbour' foreign policy to balance its giant neighbours while remaining a parliamentary democracy and a market economy.

events.title: Peaceful democratic revolution
events.summary: Demonstrations and hunger strikes in the capital lead the ruling party's leadership to resign in March 1990, ending one-party communist rule and opening the way to multiparty elections held that year.

events.title: Democratic constitution adopted
events.summary: A new constitution transforms Mongolia from a people's republic into a multiparty parliamentary democracy with an elected president and the State Great Khural, and commits the country to a market economy.

events.title: Oyu Tolgoi and the mining boom
events.summary: Agreement to develop the giant Oyu Tolgoi copper and gold deposit anchors a mining boom that multiplies Mongolia's income, reorients its economy from herding, and accelerates migration to Ulaanbaatar.

figures.name: Punsalmaagiin Ochirbat
figures.role: First democratically elected president of Mongolia
figures.blurb: Appointed president as Mongolia opened its political system in 1990 and later directly elected, he presided over the adoption of the 1992 democratic constitution and the country's transition from a one-party state to a multiparty democracy and market economy.

pullquote.text: Mongolia's renouncement of communism led to a multi-party system, a new constitution and a transition to a market economy.

figures.name: Chinggis (Genghis) Khan
figures.role: Founder of the Mongol nation and empire (proclaimed 1206)
figures.blurb: United the warring steppe tribes into one nation in 1206 under the Yassa law and launched the conquests that built the largest contiguous land empire in history.

figures.name: Kublai Khan
figures.role: Great Khan and founder of the Yuan dynasty
figures.blurb: Grandson of Chinggis Khan who conquered all of China and proclaimed the Yuan dynasty in 1271, ruling a Eurasian empire from Beijing.

figures.name: Damdin Sükhbaatar
figures.blurb: Led the 1921 People's Revolution that, with Soviet help, secured Mongolia's independence and prepared the founding of the Mongolian People's Republic in 1924.

sources.label: History of Mongolia: The ascendancy of the Manchu
Mongolia: Reform and the birth of democracy
Orkhon Valley Cultural Landscape (World Heritage List)
Mongolia: A Country Study
